epigraph.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim 
the work of His hands. Day after day they pour forth speech; night after 
night they reveal knowledge.
epigraph.quote: The book of nature is written in the language of mathematics.

content.text: {The book of nature is written in the language of mathematics.}{Galileo Galilei}

Throughout history, scientists and mathematicians have discovered formulas 
that describe the hidden patterns of creation: how objects fall, how planets 
orbit, how heat flows, how populations grow. These formulas are often 
written with multiple variables—$V = \pi r^2 h$ for the volume of a 
cylinder, $F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32$ for temperature conversion, $d = rt$ 
for distance.

In this section, we learn to rearrange such formulas by solving for any 
variable we choose. This skill is essential in science and engineering, 
where you often know some quantities and need to find others.

content.title: Literal Equation
content.content:
A **literal equation** is an equation that 
contains two or more variables. When we "solve" a literal equation, we 
isolate one variable in terms of the others.

items: The area of a trapezoid: $A = \frac{1}{2}h(b_1 + b_2)$

content.text:
Each of these relates several quantities. Depending on what information 
we have, we might need to solve for any one of them.

The same techniques we used for equations with numbers work for literal 
equations. The only difference is that our answer will be an expression 
involving other variables, not a single number.

content.title: Solving Literal Equations
content.content:
To solve a literal equation for a specified variable:

1. Identify the variable you are solving for.

2. Use inverse operations to isolate that variable, treating all 
 other letters as if they were numbers.

3. Your answer will be the variable equals an expression containing 
 the other variables.

content.title: Solving for one variable
content.problem: The formula for the perimeter of a rectangle is $P = 2l + 2w$. Solve for $w$.
content.solution:
We want to isolate $w$. Start with the formula:
\[
 P = 2l + 2w
\]

Subtract $2l$ from both sides:
$$
 P - 2l &= 2w
$$

Divide both sides by 2:
$$
 \frac{P - 2l}{2} &= w
$$

So $w = \dfrac{P - 2l}{2}$, or equivalently, $w = \dfrac{P}{2} - l$.

content.title: Temperature conversion
content.problem:
The formula $F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32$ converts Celsius to Fahrenheit. Solve 
for $C$.
content.solution:
We want to isolate $C$. Start with:
\[
 F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32
\]

Subtract 32 from both sides:
$$
 F - 32 &= \frac{9}{5}C
$$

Multiply both sides by $\frac{5}{9}$:
$$
 \frac{5}{9}(F - 32) &= C
$$

So $C = \frac{5}{9}(F - 32)$.

This is the Fahrenheit-to-Celsius conversion formula.

content.title: Simple interest
content.problem:
The simple interest formula is $I = Prt$, where $I$ is interest, $P$ is 
principal, $r$ is rate (as a decimal), and $t$ is time. Solve for $P$.
content.solution:
We want to isolate $P$. The right side is $P \cdot r \cdot t$—a product.

Divide both sides by $rt$:
$$
 \frac{I}{rt} &= P
$$

So $P = \dfrac{I}{rt}$.

Note: This assumes $r \neq 0$ and $t \neq 0$.

content.text: Some formulas require multiple steps or careful attention to grouping.

content.title: Solving for a variable in a sum
content.problem:
The surface area of a rectangular prism is $S = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh$. Solve 
for $h$.
content.solution:
We want to isolate $h$. First, identify the terms containing $h$:
\[
 S = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh
\]

The terms $2lh$ and $2wh$ both contain $h$. Rearrange by subtracting $2lw$:
$$
 S - 2lw &= 2lh + 2wh
$$

Factor out $h$ from the right side:
$$
 S - 2lw &= h(2l + 2w)
$$

Divide both sides by $(2l + 2w)$:
$$
 \frac{S - 2lw}{2l + 2w} &= h
$$

So $h = \dfrac{S - 2lw}{2l + 2w}$, or we can factor the denominator: 
$h = \dfrac{S - 2lw}{2(l + w)}$.

content.title: The quadratic formula preparation
content.solution:
We have $x$ on both sides. Gather the $x$-terms:
\[
 ax + b = cx + d
\]

Subtract $cx$ from both sides:
$$
 ax - cx + b &= d
$$

Factor out $x$:
$$
 x(a - c) + b &= d
$$

Subtract $b$:
$$
 x(a - c) &= d - b
$$

Divide by $(a - c)$:
$$
 x &= \frac{d - b}{a - c}
$$

Note: This assumes $a \neq c$.

content.title: Area of a trapezoid
content.problem: The area of a trapezoid is $A = \frac{1}{2}h(b_1 + b_2)$. Solve for $b_1$.
content.solution:
First, eliminate the fraction by multiplying both sides by 2:
$$
 2A &= h(b_1 + b_2)
$$

Divide both sides by $h$:
$$
 \frac{2A}{h} &= b_1 + b_2
$$

Subtract $b_2$:
$$
 \frac{2A}{h} - b_2 &= b_1
$$

So $b_1 = \dfrac{2A}{h} - b_2$.

content.text:
Rearranging formulas is not just an algebraic exercise—it's a practical 
skill used constantly in science, engineering, and everyday life.

content.title: Using a rearranged formula
content.problem:
The formula for the area of a circle is $A = \pi r^2$. 

(a) Solve for $r$.

(b) Find the radius of a circle whose area is 50 square centimeters. 
Use $\pi \approx 3.14$.
content.solution:
(a) Start with $A = \pi r^2$. Divide by $\pi$:
\[
 \frac{A}{\pi} = r^2
\]

Take the square root (radius is positive):
\[
 r = \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}
\]

(b) Substitute $A = 50$:
$$
 r &= \sqrt{\frac{50}{3.14}} \\
 r &= \sqrt{15.92...} \\
 r &\approx 3.99 \text{ cm}
$$

The radius is approximately 4 cm.

content.title: Physics: velocity and acceleration
content.problem:
The formula $v = v_0 + at$ relates final velocity $v$, initial velocity 
$v_0$, acceleration $a$, and time $t$.

(a) Solve for $a$.

(b) A car accelerates from 10 m/s to 25 m/s in 5 seconds. What is the 
acceleration?
content.solution:
(a) Start with $v = v_0 + at$. Subtract $v_0$:
$$
 v - v_0 &= at
$$

Divide by $t$:
$$
 a &= \frac{v - v_0}{t}
$$

(b) Substitute $v = 25$, $v_0 = 10$, and $t = 5$:
$$
 a &= \frac{25 - 10}{5} = \frac{15}{5} = 3 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

The acceleration is 3 m/s².

content.title: Finance: compound interest
content.problem:
The compound interest formula for annual compounding is $A = P(1 + r)^t$, 
where $A$ is the final amount, $P$ is the principal, $r$ is the annual 
interest rate, and $t$ is the number of years.

Solve for $P$.
content.solution:
Start with $A = P(1 + r)^t$. Divide both sides by $(1 + r)^t$:
$$
 P &= \frac{A}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

This tells us how much to invest now to reach a target amount later.

content.title: Isaac Newton (1643--1727)
content.content:
Isaac Newton's laws of motion, first published in 1687, use literal 
equations to describe how objects move. His second law, $F = ma$, 
relates force, mass, and acceleration. This single equation can be 
rearranged to solve for any variable: $m = \frac{F}{a}$ or 
$a = \frac{F}{m}$. Newton recognized that mathematics was the language 
in which God wrote the laws of nature, writing: "This most beautiful 
system of the sun, planets, and comets could only proceed from the 
counsel and dominion of an intelligent and powerful Being."

marginNotes.text:
For since the creation of the world God's 
invisible qualities—His eternal power and divine nature—have been 
clearly seen, being understood from what has been made.

It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; 
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

marginNotes.author: Johannes Kepler
marginNotes.text:
I was merely thinking God's thoughts after 
Him.
